codeSnapshot.label: bayesian_optimization_hpo.py
codeSnapshot.code:
# Bayesian Optimization for Hyperparameter Optimization
# Surrogate model (GP) + Acquisition function → next point to evaluate
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm
from sklearn.gaussian_process import GaussianProcessRegressor
from sklearn.gaussian_process.kernels import Matern

# ---- Gaussian Process Surrogate ----
# After n evaluations: posterior μₙ(x), σₙ(x)
# Acquisition function guides next query point

def expected_improvement(X, gp, y_best, xi=0.01):
    """
    Expected Improvement (EI) acquisition function.
    EI(x) = E[max(f(x) - f*, 0)]
           = (μ(x) - f* - ξ)·Φ(Z) + σ(x)·φ(Z)  if σ > 0
           = 0                                       if σ = 0
    where Z = (μ(x) - f* - ξ) / σ(x)
    """
    mu, sigma = gp.predict(X, return_std=True)
    sigma = sigma.reshape(-1, 1)
    mu = mu.reshape(-1, 1)
    Z = (mu - y_best - xi) / (sigma + 1e-9)
    ei = (mu - y_best - xi) * norm.cdf(Z) + sigma * norm.pdf(Z)
    ei[sigma <= 0.0] = 0.0
    return ei.ravel()

def upper_confidence_bound(X, gp, kappa=2.576):
    """UCB(x) = μ(x) + κ·σ(x)  [exploitation + exploration]"""
    mu, sigma = gp.predict(X, return_std=True)
    return mu + kappa * sigma

# Bayesian optimization loop
def bayesian_optimize(objective, bounds, n_init=5, n_iter=20):
    """
    bounds: list of (low, high) for each hyperparameter
    objective: f(x) → scalar (higher is better)
    """
    d = len(bounds)
    # Phase 1: random initialisation (Latin hypercube)
    X = np.array([[np.random.uniform(lo, hi) for lo, hi in bounds]
                  for _ in range(n_init)])
    y = np.array([objective(x) for x in X])

    # GP surrogate (Matern kernel — standard for HPO)
    kernel = Matern(nu=2.5)  # once differentiable, good for HPO landscapes
    gp = GaussianProcessRegressor(kernel=kernel, alpha=1e-6, normalize_y=True,
                                   n_restarts_optimizer=5)
    for i in range(n_iter):
        gp.fit(X, y)
        # Maximise acquisition function over dense grid (or with L-BFGS-B)
        candidates = np.array([[np.random.uniform(lo, hi) for lo, hi in bounds]
                                for _ in range(10000)])
        ei = expected_improvement(candidates, gp, y.max())
        x_next = candidates[np.argmax(ei)]
        y_next = objective(x_next)
        X = np.vstack([X, x_next])
        y = np.append(y, y_next)
        print(f'Iter {i+1:2d}: x={x_next}, y={y_next:.4f}, best={y.max():.4f}')
    return X[np.argmax(y)], y.max()

# TPE (Tree-structured Parzen Estimator) — used by Optuna, Hyperopt
# Models p(x|good) and p(x|bad) separately, selects x maximising p(x|good)/p(x|bad)
import optuna
optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)

def optuna_objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float('lr', 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)
    wd = trial.suggest_float('weight_decay', 1e-6, 1e-2, log=True)
    # ... train model and return validation score
    return -lr * wd  # placeholder

study = optuna.create_study(direction='maximize')  # TPE sampler by default
study.optimize(optuna_objective, n_trials=30)
print(f'Best: {study.best_params}')

eli3: Bayesian optimization is a smart hyperparameter search that learns from previous evaluations. It builds a surrogate model (usually a Gaussian Process) that predicts performance for any hyperparameter configuration, then uses this model to decide where to search next.

The key idea: instead of trying random configurations, it intelligently balances exploration (trying unknown regions) and exploitation (trying near the current best). An acquisition function captures this tradeoff — Expected Improvement (EI) calculates 'how much better could this configuration be vs my current best?'

Bayesian optimization is much more efficient than random search when training is expensive (hours per configuration). With 20 evaluations, it often matches random search with 200 evaluations. It's the standard approach for tuning deep learning training runs, NLP model training, and expensive scientific simulations.

whenWhy.title: Expensive objective functions (few evaluations affordable)
whenWhy.body: When each model evaluation takes hours (large neural network, protein folding simulation), you can't afford hundreds of random trials. Bayesian optimization achieves near-optimal hyperparameters in 20-50 evaluations for most problems. Snoek et al. (2012) showed that 30 BO evaluations outperformed manual expert tuning and random search on CNN/SVM hyperparameter optimization tasks. Standard applications: neural network architecture + training hyperparameter search, drug discovery (expensive lab tests), and engineering simulations.

whenWhy.title: Continuous hyperparameter spaces with complex landscapes
whenWhy.body: Gaussian Process surrogates model the full hyperparameter landscape including correlations and non-monotone behavior (e.g., learning rate and batch size interact — optimal LR depends on batch size). Random search treats each hyperparameter independently; BO learns these correlations from past evaluations. This makes BO especially effective when the optimal hyperparameter configuration depends on complex interactions, such as the relationship between momentum, learning rate, and weight decay in AdamW.

whenWhy.title: TPE: Bayesian optimization for mixed spaces
whenWhy.body: Gaussian Process BO struggles with high-dimensional (>20D) and mixed (continuous + discrete + categorical) spaces due to GP kernel computation cost. TPE (Tree-structured Parzen Estimator, Bergstra et al., 2011) models the conditional distribution p(x|good) and p(x|bad) as kernel density estimates, then picks x that maximises p(x|good)/p(x|bad). TPE handles mixed spaces naturally and scales to higher dimensions. Hyperopt and Optuna (default sampler) use TPE — the most widely used BO variant in practice.

deepDiveIntro: Bayesian optimization (Snoek et al., 2012) fits a Gaussian Process surrogate model to past evaluation results. This surrogate gives both a predicted performance and an uncertainty estimate for any untried configuration. An acquisition function — most commonly Expected Improvement — uses these two quantities to decide where to search next, balancing trying near known-good configurations with exploring uncertain regions. [1]

The Matérn-2.5 kernel is the standard choice for the GP because it assumes the performance landscape is somewhat rough — which matches reality for neural network hyperparameters — rather than perfectly smooth as the RBF kernel assumes. Integrating over the GP's own hyperparameters (like length-scale) using MCMC prevents the surrogate from overfitting to limited observations. [1]

deepDive.heading: Expected Improvement Acquisition Function
deepDive.body: Expected Improvement asks: how much better than the current best result might this configuration be, on average? It is large when a configuration is either predicted to be very good (high mean) or when the model is uncertain about it (high uncertainty) — naturally trading off exploitation and exploration. A small exploration parameter controls how aggressively the search pushes into uncertain territory beyond the current best. EI is zero wherever the model is confident the result will be worse than the best seen so far. [1]

deepDive.heading: TPE: Tree-structured Parzen Estimator
deepDive.body: TPE (Bergstra et al., 2011) takes a different approach to Bayesian optimization: instead of modeling predicted performance directly, it separates past evaluations into 'good' (top 15%) and 'bad' results and fits separate density models to each group. The next configuration to try is chosen by maximizing the ratio of the good density to the bad density — i.e., find the region that looks like good results but unlike bad ones. The tree-structured part means TPE naturally handles conditional hyperparameters (where parameter B only applies if parameter A has a certain value). Hyperopt and Optuna use TPE by default. [2]

deepDive.heading: SMAC and Random Forest Surrogates
deepDive.body: SMAC (Hutter et al., 2011) replaces the Gaussian Process surrogate with a random forest, which handles mixed spaces (categorical, conditional, and continuous hyperparameters) and scales to high-dimensional search spaces that GPs struggle with. Prediction variance across the trees serves as the uncertainty estimate for the acquisition function. SMAC is the engine behind Auto-sklearn and excels at algorithm configuration tasks involving many interacting hyperparameters. It can also be warm-started with configurations that worked well on similar past datasets, dramatically accelerating convergence on a new problem. [1]

youtubeLabel: Bayesian Optimization Explained — Gaussian Processes and Acquisition Functions
takeaways: EI(x)=(μ−f*−ξ)·Φ(Z)+σ·φ(Z) — balances exploitation (high μ) and exploration (high σ). [1]
GP surrogate: Matérn-2.5 kernel standard; posterior μₙ,σₙ updated after each evaluation. [1]
BO achieves in 20-50 evaluations what random search needs 200+ for expensive objectives. [1]
TPE: models p(x|good)/p(x|bad) via KDE — handles categorical and conditional spaces. [2]
SMAC uses random forest surrogate: scales to high-dimensional mixed spaces (used in Auto-sklearn). [1]

edgeCases.q: When does Bayesian optimization underperform random search?
edgeCases.a: Bayesian optimization underperforms random search when: (1) Cheap evaluation budget (< 20 evaluations) — GP fitting and acquisition maximization overhead dominates. (2) High-dimensional space (>20 dimensions) — GP kernel matrix computation is O(n³) in observations and the GP struggles to model high-D spaces reliably. (3) Noisy objective — GP is designed for noiseless observations; high noise (from stochastic training) requires a noise-aware GP (observation noise σ_n > 0), which is available in sklearn GaussianProcessRegressor(alpha=σ_n²). (4) Very fast evaluations — if each trial takes seconds, the overhead of BO (GP fitting, acquisition maximization) is comparable to the trial cost; random search is simpler and equally fast. For deep learning: BO is worthwhile when single training runs take >1 hour.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle parallel Bayesian optimization (batch BO)?
edgeCases.a: Standard BO is sequential: fit GP, maximise acquisition, evaluate, repeat. In parallel settings (multiple GPU workers), you want to query a batch of points simultaneously. Batch BO approaches: (1) Local Penalisation (González et al., 2016): after selecting x₁, penalise the acquisition function near x₁ before selecting x₂ — discourages the batch from clustering around one point. (2) Thompson Sampling: for each parallel worker, sample a random function from the GP posterior and maximise it — this is equivalent to BO with diversity. (3) Hallucination / Kriging Believer: assume pending evaluations return μ(x_pending) and add them to the GP — allows selecting future points without waiting. Optuna supports parallelism natively with the optuna.Study multiprocessing backend.

sources.label: Snoek, Larochelle & Adams (2012) — Practical Bayesian Optimization of Machine Learning Algorithms, NeurIPS 2012
Bergstra et al. (2011) — Algorithms for Hyper-Parameter Optimization (TPE), NeurIPS 2011
